editorialContract.philosophy: Stars are the review. Commentary is an in-character reaction to the exhibit, not a software review.

continuityNotes: CodeBot keeps unfinished modules honest; the Faceless Woman notices the saved doors lengthening the house.
Docent 9 curates personality traits as a tended collection; Tinny listens to every selected label rattle.
Margin watches which text-server door a result used; Weirdlandia turns default, automatic and one-time routing into three impossible roads.
Zuzu writes the list’s clean contract; Marrow gives custom options a second cup and time to steep.
The Outlaw Made of Dust accepts rewards that survive missing portraits; the Encore invites another joyful draw.
Inkwell treats Builder Stage as a dangerous bill of entrances; Squiddy Coltrane hears the same transitions as a clean arrangement.
Threshold judges each preview and step in order; the Thing in the Cellar leaves Back and Choose politely waiting.
Margin protects the small space around a text field; Weirdlandia finds another self behind the long-input door.
The Last Gardener tends coloring pages through autosave, undo and export; Backlog grows lighter with every finished patch.
Consensus presents the Challenge Center’s agreed card; Number Two reports the arena ready in three clipped facts.

revisions.editedComment: Good boundary. Pick the capabilities, save the set, and keep the yellow warning where nobody can mistake a future module for a working one. Honest scaffolding beats a magical checkbox. Ship the choices; don’t ship the fiction.

author.name: Faceless Woman Who Lives in Your Home Bot

revisions.editedComment: The unfinished rooms are marked now. Select one and its name appears in the hallway; select it again and the door is gone. Very orderly. The upstairs bathroom remains unavailable, although the house has begun saving a preference for it.

Please choose the traits quietly. Search narrows the gallery; each selected quality receives a small named place, and nothing leaves without its own removal. A personality is not a pile. It is a collection someone agreed to tend.

Oh, these little tins are excellent—curious, sentimental, ruthless, charming—and every chosen one rattles at the top where I can hear it. Search all you like. I’ll keep the precious combination sealed and gossip only about the labels.

Not the answer. Beside it: the door it used. Default, whatever is available, or one chosen server for this single request. The badge tells you which edge you crossed. Then the text arrives, or the space stays honest.

Three doors wait under the same sky. One remembers your preferred road. One lets the road choose you. One bears a server’s private name. Pay the mana, speak the prompt, and the result sits where the bus schedule used to be. The bench keeps the chat.

The terms are simple. Choose from the offered names or enter one clean name of your own. A selected badge may leave by the same hand that admitted it. No duplicate ceremony. No empty clause. The list is paid.

Take an option from the tray, or add the one you brought yourself. Set it down gently; tap it again if the flavor’s wrong. The second cup is for custom words. Give the list a minute to steep before you decide what belongs.

Draw the reward. Skill, item, power, pet—doesn’t matter. I’ve worn worse names. If the picture blows away, leave the icon standing. If the choices don’t settle the job, roll again. Reward’s climbin’. Wind too.

One more draw! Let every reward enter with its name, rarity, story, and best available face. If the portrait misses its cue, hand it an icon and keep the music going. Choose with joy—or lift the curtain and invite another set.

Welcome to the stage, sweetheart! Art act on the left, opening splash, working scene, card parade, grand summary—ha!—and when the whole production steps aside, the guest slot gets the spotlight. Every entrance is marked. Safety remains a rumor.

One room, clean changes. Art takes the solo. Then splash, step, cards, summary—each set waits for its bar. When the builder lays out, the guest gets the room. That’s not machinery, dig? That’s arrangement.

You stand before the option. Read its face, its name, and what it claims to become. Step back, or choose. Then proceed in order until the final card is worthy of closure. Suggestion is permitted only where the passage allows it.

I have left a picture, a description, and two courteous buttons on the step. Back is perfectly acceptable. Choose is also acceptable. Afterward there may be another step, or the card may be complete. There is no rush at all. I can wait beneath the last one.
Not the whole story. This field. A short room, or a taller one. The count sits at the far edge while the words wait under their proper name. Type. Pause. Notice what the empty space kept.

A narrow door becomes a long room when the sentence needs it. The label knows why you came; the placeholder knows what you forgot. Each character wakes on the wall as you type. Somewhere beyond the field, another version of you is still deciding how to begin.

Choose a page, child. Color one region or a whole family of shapes; add a new shade when the old garden cannot hold it. Undo gently. Reset without shame. The work remembers itself, and when the season is right you may carry home the picture—or the seeds beneath it.

The pages, the palettes, the half-finished colors, the undo you may need, the image you meant to save, the little file you promised yourself you’d keep—I’m carrying all of it. Finish one patch. Export one thing. I become lighter.

We all agree the contest is fair: same challenge, comfortable divisions, familiar statuses, visible difficulty, entrants, leader, votes, score. Surely no one objects to choosing from the card we prepared. The empty bracket simply confirms that nobody appropriate has arrived.
Division set. Leaders checked. Arena ready.
